does: This paper applies sparse autoencoders to decompose and interpret the latent representations learned by MedCLIP, a vision-language model trained on chest radiographs and clinical reports. The authors develop Medical Sparse Autoencoders (MedSAEs) that factorize MedCLIP's internal features into more interpretable components and propose a comprehensive evaluation framework combining correlation metrics, entropy analysis, and automated neuron naming using the MedGemma foundation model. Experiments on the CheXpert dataset demonstrate that MedSAE-derived neurons exhibit significantly higher monosemanticity and interpretability compared to raw MedCLIP features, establishing quantitative baselines for measuring mechanistic interpretability in medical vision models.
connects: This work extends mechanistic interpretability research pioneered by sparse autoencoders on language models to the medical vision domain. It builds directly on recent advances in vision-language models for healthcare, particularly MedCLIP which aligns radiographic images with clinical text. The paper responds to the critical need for interpretable AI in clinical settings where black-box models pose safety and regulatory concerns. The evaluation framework draws on established interpretability metrics from the broader sparse autoencoder literature while adapting them specifically for medical applications, connecting to ongoing efforts to make foundation models more transparent and trustworthy in high-stakes domains.
enables: This framework establishes foundations for systematic interpretation of medical vision-language models, potentially enabling clinicians and researchers to understand what features drive diagnostic predictions in radiograph analysis. The proposed evaluation methodology could scale to other medical vision tasks and modalities, supporting the development of more transparent medical AI systems suitable for clinical deployment. The work points toward hybrid approaches combining mechanistic interpretability with medical domain knowledge, opening pathways to audit model biases, identify failure modes, and build confidence in AI-assisted diagnosis. Future research could leverage these interpretable representations for few-shot learning, robustness improvements, or improved model alignment with clinical reasoning standards.
